ucs.title: [LLM] APT41 web-app exploitation → ASPXSpy/China Chopper webshell post-exploit recon
ucs.description: Hunts APT41/BARIUM's signature initial-access chain: exploit an internet-facing web app (Citrix NetScaler CVE-2019-19781, ProxyLogon, Log4Shell, Atlassian Confluence) and drop an ASPXSpy or China Chopper webshell, then immediately run host/AD recon under the IIS/Tomcat process tree. Microsoft attributes this exact pattern to Brass Typhoon, and Mandiant reported it in the 2022 USAONE intrusion and the 2024 DUSTTRAP campaign.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdline values(Processes.process_name) as child from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name IN ("w3wp.exe","tomcat*.exe","java.exe","httpd.exe","nginx.exe","javaw.exe","PhpCGI.exe") AND Processes.process_name IN ("cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","ipconfig.exe","systeminfo.exe","tasklist.exe","netstat.exe","wmic.exe","nltest.exe","quser.exe","reg.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe") by host Processes.user Processes.parent_process_name Processes.parent_process_path Processes.dest _time span=1h | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | rename host as dest_host | eventstats dc(child) as distinct_recon_bins by dest_host parent_process_name | where distinct_recon_bins>=3 OR match(cmdline,"(?i)(whoami\s*/all|net\s+(user|group|localgroup)\s+/dom|nltest\s+/dclist|systeminfo|ipconfig\s*/all|tasklist\s*/v|netstat\s+-ano|reg\s+save.*sam|certutil\s+-urlcache|bitsadmin\s+/transfer)") | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | table firstTime dest_host user parent_process_name parent_process_path child cmdline distinct_recon_bins
ucs.kql:
let webProcs = dynamic(["w3wp.exe","tomcat.exe","tomcat9.exe","java.exe","javaw.exe","httpd.exe","nginx.exe","php-cgi.exe"]);
let reconBins = dynamic(["cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","ipconfig.exe","systeminfo.exe","tasklist.exe","netstat.exe","wmic.exe","nltest.exe","quser.exe","reg.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe"]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ (webProcs)
| where FileName in~ (reconBins)
| extend recon = ProcessCommandLine
| where recon matches regex @"(?i)(whoami\s*/all|net\s+(user|group|localgroup)\s+/dom|nltest\s+/dclist|systeminfo|ipconfig\s*/all|tasklist\s*/v|netstat\s+-ano|reg\s+save.*sam|certutil\s+-urlcache|bitsadmin\s+/transfer)"
   or FileName in~ ("whoami.exe","systeminfo.exe","nltest.exe")
| summarize recon_cmds=make_set(ProcessCommandLine,25), distinct_bins=dcount(FileName), first=min(Timestamp), last=max(Timestamp) by DeviceId, DeviceName, AccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessFolderPath
| where distinct_bins >= 2 or array_length(recon_cmds) >= 3
| order by last desc
ucs.rationale: APT41 is one of the few crews that consistently pairs N-day web-app exploitation with hands-on-keyboard recon executed *as the web service account* within minutes of webshell drop. Legit IIS/Tomcat workers almost never spawn cmd.exe/whoami/nltest — and combined recon (e.g. nltest /dclist + reg save sam + bitsadmin) under w3wp is a near-pathognomonic signature documented in Mandiant M-Trends 2023 a

ucs.title: [LLM] APT41 DUSTTRAP/DEADEYE DLL side-loading via signed third-party binaries
ucs.description: Hunts the Winnti/APT41 hallmark of dropping a legitimately-signed vendor EXE (often from a gaming, AV, or graphics vendor) into a writable directory alongside a malicious same-named DLL (e.g. DUSTPAN, DUSTTRAP, ScatterBee, CROSSWALK loader). Mandiant's July-2024 APT41 report (Global, Italy/Spain/Taiwan/Turkey) and CrowdStrike's Wicked Panda profile both call this sideloading pattern out as the group's persistent loader-of-choice across a decade.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdline values(Processes.parent_process_name) as parent from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_path IN ("*\\ProgramData\\*","*\\Users\\Public\\*","*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*","*\\PerfLogs\\*","*\\Windows\\Tasks\\*","*\\Windows\\Debug\\*") AND Processes.process_name IN ("AUG.exe","BDReinit.exe","IMECMNT.EXE","MsMpEng.exe","vmnat.exe","vmtoolsd.exe","GUP.exe","AvastSvc.exe","NvSmartMax.exe","NvContainer.exe","hpqhvind.exe","oleview.exe","sgrmbroker.exe") by host Processes.user Processes.process_name Processes.process_path Processes.parent_process_name Processes.process_id Processes.process_hash | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | rename host as dest_host process_id as pid | join type=inner dest_host pid [| tstats summariesonly=true values(Filesystem.file_name) as dlls from datamodel=Endpoint.Filesystem where Filesystem.file_name="*.dll" Filesystem.action="created" by host Filesystem.process_id | `drop_dm_object_name(Filesystem)` | rename host as dest_host process_id as pid] | where mvcount(dlls)>=1 | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | table firstTime dest_host user process_name process_path parent_process_name dlls cmdline
ucs.kql:
let suspectDirs = dynamic([@"\programdata\", @"\users\public\", @"\appdata\local\temp\", @"\perflogs\", @"\windows\tasks\", @"\windows\debug\"]);
let apt41SideloadHosts = dynamic(["aug.exe","bdreinit.exe","imecmnt.exe","msmpeng.exe","vmnat.exe","vmtoolsd.exe","gup.exe","avastsvc.exe","nvsmartmax.exe","nvcontainer.exe","hpqhvind.exe","oleview.exe","sgrmbroker.exe"]);
let sideloadProcs =
    DeviceProcessEvents
    | where Timestamp > ago(14d)
    | where tolower(FileName) in (apt41SideloadHosts)
    | where suspectDirs has_any (tolower(FolderPath))
    | project Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName, AccountName, ProcessId=ProcessId, FileName, FolderPath, SHA256, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName;
let sideloadDlls =
    DeviceImageLoadEvents
    | where Timestamp > ago(14d)
    | where FileName endswith ".dll"
    | where suspectDirs has_any (tolower(FolderPath))
    | where InitiatingProcessVersionInfoCompanyName !in~ ("Microsoft Corporation","Microsoft Windows")
    | project Timestamp, DeviceId, ProcessId=InitiatingProcessId, DllName=FileName, DllPath=FolderPath, DllSha256=SHA256, SignerCompany=InitiatingProcessVersionInfoCompanyName;
sideloadProcs
| join kind=inner (sideloadDlls) on DeviceId, ProcessId
| join kind=leftouter (DeviceFileCertificateInfo | project DllSha256=SHA256, IsSigned, IsTrusted, Signer) on DllSha256
| where IsSigned == false or IsTrusted == false or isempty(IsSigned)
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, FileName, FolderPath, DllName, DllPath, DllSha256, IsSigned, IsTrusted, Signer, ProcessCommandLine
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.rationale: DLL side-loading from a signed-vendor decoy is the single most consistent APT41/Winnti TTP across BARIUM, Blackfly, and Brass Typhoon clusters — same trick used since the 2014 Winnti gaming-industry intrusions through DUSTTRAP in 2024. Pinning the hunt to (a) the exact vendor binaries Mandiant/CrowdStrike have publicly named as APT41 sideload hosts, (b) execution from non-installed paths (ProgramD
